\[Some content here\]

Ilych John Edward Tuvilla King Phillip Tacsagon  
Michael Anthony Polong

June 2025

I. Tuvilla; K. Tacsagon, Michael Anthony Polong “Development of an Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System:  
Optimizing Crop Planning, Financial Forecasting, and Market Supply Chain Governance,” Unpublished Undergraduate Thesis, Bachelor of Science in Information Systems,College of  
Information and Communications Technology, West Visayas State University, Iloilo City, Philippines, 2026\.

Abstract

Effective agricultural planning is vital to the farming

sector—from crop selection to market distribution—to ensure

that farmers achieve economic stability and communities avoid the negative impacts of oversupply. This study proposes the  
development of an Integrated Agricultural Decision Support

System that analyzes real-time crop saturation and recommends optimal planting strategies and financial forecasting to  
mitigate the risks of local market gluts. The research aims to provide a predictive Information Systems solution that bridges the data deficit between farmers and the Municipal Agriculture Office to optimize harvest synchronization, digitize agronomic monitoring, and support supply chain governance with  
evidence-based analytics.

Keywords: crop planning, financial forecasting, market saturation, supply chain governance, decision support, agricultural information systems

CHAPTER 1 INTRODUCTION TO THE STUDY

The Information Deficit and Informal Agricultural Practices In many developing agricultural municipalities like Tubungan, the prevailing information systems are largely informal,  
traditional, and heavily reliant on manual processes. At the farmer level, decision making regarding crop selection and  
planting schedules is primarily heuristic based on historical memory, weather lore, or the imitation of neighboring farms  
\[1\]. This traditional "calendar planting" approach lacks real time data integration, leaving farmers vulnerable to localized oversupply and the subsequent depreciation of crop market  
value \[1\], \[2\]. Financial forecasting is similarly informal, often calculated mentally or recorded in physical ledgers.  
This makes it difficult for farmers to accurately project

their Return on Investment (ROI) or assess the financial risk of input costs (e.g., fertilizers, seeds, labor) before the  
planting season begins.

Economic Repercussions and Supply Chain Volatility This lack

of synchronization creates immense financial vulnerability. In agricultural economics, this phenomenon is often explained by the "Bullwhip Effect" and the "Cobweb Model," where delayed  
information causes extreme fluctuations in supply and demand \[3\]. Because many farmers end up planting the same crops at  
the same time without knowing the market's capacity, the local market frequently experiences severe oversupply, which causes prices to drop significantly upon harvest \[2\]. Post harvest  
losses in the Philippines often reach up to 40% for

perishables, largely driven by these logistical failures and the lack of immediate market access for surplus produce \[4\].

Institutional Limitations and Existing Technological Gaps At

the institutional level, Local Government Units (LGUs) and the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO) typically utilize  
traditional data gathering methods. The baseline for

agricultural monitoring is often tied to paper based surveys and manual data entry into the Registry System for Basic  
Sectors in Agriculture (RSBSA). Because data collection and consolidation take time, the MAO lacks a real time, dynamic

dashboard to monitor active planting trends and is unable to issue timely advisories to farmers regarding potential market oversaturation \[5\].

Several technological solutions have been developed to address agricultural inefficiencies, though most function as  
standalone tools rather than fully integrated ecosystems. Locally, the Department of Science and Technology (DOST)  
developed Project SARAI (Smarter Approaches to Reinvigorate Agriculture as an Industry in the Philippines). While SARAI  
provides crop advisories and yield estimates using geographic information systems (GIS), its primary focus is on maximizing yield and managing weather risks rather than market supply  
chain governance and financial forecasting for individual farmers \[6\].

Geospatial Governance and Predictive Analytics Recent academic studies highlight the critical role of predictive analytics  
and Spatial Decision Support Systems (SDSS) in bridging these gaps. Decision Support Systems are most effective when they  
combine agronomic data with economic models; farmers require

clear, tangible financial forecasts to justify the change from their traditional practices \[7\]. Furthermore, establishing a  
centralized data sharing platform at the municipal level

allows authorities to apply predictive analytics to upcoming harvest volumes \[3\]. By mapping planned crop distributions  
geospatially, LGUs can visually identify clusters of similar crops, moving away from static record keeping to dynamic  
monitoring \[8\]. Incorporating these geospatial insights

alongside machine learning predictive models enhances land use optimization, ensuring that crop varieties are distributed  
efficiently based on both soil suitability and market demand \[9\], \[10\].

The Proposed System To address these systemic issues, this

study proposes the development of an integrated Agricultural Decision Support System for Tubungan. By combining the  
predictive yield modeling discussed in spatial studies with

the economic governance principles of supply chain management, the proposed system effectively bridges the gap between  
agricultural production and market absorption. The system will

integrate planting intent, input costs, and disaster reports

through a unified framework and apply predictive algorithms to recommend optimal planting strategies. Through the integration of a financial forecasting engine, a geospatial Supply Chain  
Dashboard, and a Post Harvest Variance Redistribution module for surplus management, this study seeks to provide the MAO and the Farmers Association with an evidence based tool that optimizes crop profitability, streamlines disaster damage  
assessment, and actively prevents local market saturation.

*Fig. 1\. Conceptual Framework*

*![][image1]*

**Fig. 1\.** Figure caption

Figure 1 shows an Input–Process–Output (IPO) conceptual

framework illustrating the flow of data within the proposed Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System. In the Input phase, the system collects essential agricultural and market  
data, including historical crop prices, yield per hectare records, seasonal and land suitability information, farmer

land area, market demand data, farm activity logs, and disaster reports submitted by farmers.

These data proceed to the Process phase, where the system

performs core analytical operations such as data integration, predictive crop saturation analysis, crop recommendation  
generation, financial profit simulation, supply chain

forecasting, and digital logbook processing. The system also aggregates harvest schedules and evaluates disaster damage reports to support agricultural monitoring and governance.

Finally, the Output phase converts the processed data into decision-support information, including optimal crop planting  
strategies, projected profit forecasts, harvest timeline projections, supply chain monitoring dashboards for the  
Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO), oversupply alerts, and digital farm records to assist farmers and administrators in  
making data-driven agricultural decisions.

**TABLE I** SAMPLE TABLE CAPTION

| Sample Table Header | Sample Table Header |
| :---- | :---- |
| Sample Table Content | Sample Table Content |

Objectives of the Study

To develop a recommendation agricultural decision support

system that optimizes planting strategies and financial

forecasting, synchronizes harvest supply to mitigate market saturation, and digitizes agronomic monitoring and incident recording.

Specifically, it aims to:

1. 	To develop a recommendation module that analyzes land suitability, seasonal constraints, and crop saturation to recommend optimal planting strategies, including single-crop and intercropping (mix-and-match) scenarios—while

   generating automated harvest timelines to mitigate the risk of local oversupply

2. 	To implement a financial modeling tool that recording profit margins based on

   projected market prices and land area, allowing farmers to compare the economic viability of

   recommended crops versus saturated crops (Risk Assessment), while tracking actual input costs

(fertilizers, labor) to calculate final Profit and Loss (P\&L) post-harvest.

3. To engineer a supply chain dashboard that provides the MAO and Associations with a forward-looking

   supply projection, utilizing harvest synchronization logic to prevent simultaneous dumping of produce and automatically identifying alternative market

   channels or Association buy-back programs for predicted surpluses.

4. 	To establish a comprehensive digital logbook for recording agronomic events (such as fertilizer

   application) and an incident reporting mechanism that allows farmers to report 

   calamity-induced losses immediately, streamlining the verification process for government subsidy

   Allocation. And To evaluate the acceptability and performance of the developed system in terms of functional suitability, usability, and reliability using the ISO/IEC 25010 standard. 

Significance of the Study

This study will benefit several stakeholders within the agricultural ecosystem:

Farmers \- The system will assist farmers in making informed, data-driven crop planning decisions through optimal planting recommendations (single or intercropping) and automated harvest timelines. Furthermore, the financial modeling tool will allow them to assess economic risks, compare crop viability, and track actual input costs for an accurate Profit and Loss (P\&L) assessment. Crucially, the digital logbook and incident reporting mechanism will empower them to immediately report calamity-induced losses, accelerating their access to government subsidies.

Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO)- The MAO will gain access to a forward-looking supply chain dashboard that projects harvest schedules and visualizes supply trends. This enables administrators to utilize harvest synchronization logic to proactively mitigate local market saturation. Additionally, the system streamlines the verification process for disaster claims, allowing the MAO to allocate government subsidies more efficiently and accurately.

Farmers Associations and Cooperatives \- The system will enable associations to monitor production levels among their members using forward-looking supply projections. By predicting potential surpluses, associations can rapidly organize collective mitigation strategies, such as initiating targeted surplus buy-back programs or identifying alternative market distribution channels before simultaneous dumping occurs.

Researchers and Academics **\-** The study will contribute to existing literature on agricultural decision support systems, demonstrating the practical application of functional suitability, usability, and reliability metrics (based on the ISO/IEC 25010 standard) in evaluating agricultural software. It highlights the role of digital incident reporting and harvest synchronization in improving agricultural governance.

Future Developer \- The system architecture, recommendation algorithms, and integration methodologies used in this research can serve as a robust reference for future developers aiming to build localized, highly specific agricultural supply chain and financial tracking software.

Delimitation of the Study

This study focuses on the development, implementation, and evaluation of an Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System designed primarily for small-to-medium-scale farmers, local Farmers Associations, and the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO).

The scope of the system is strictly limited to the development of the following key modules:

1. Recommendation Module: Analyzes land suitability, seasonal constraints, and crop saturation to suggest single-crop or intercropping strategies and generate automated harvest timelines.  
2. Financial Modeling Tool: Compares the economic viability of recommended versus saturated crops (Risk Assessment) and calculates final post-harvest Profit and Loss (P\&L) based on projected market prices and actual input costs (labor, fertilizers).  
3. Supply Chain Dashboard: A platform for the MAO and Associations featuring forward-looking supply projections, harvest synchronization logic, and automated identification of alternative markets or buy-back programs.  
4. Digital Logbook & Incident Reporting: A centralized mechanism for tracking agronomic events and reporting 

   calamity-induced losses to streamline subsidy allocation.

The study will utilize historical agricultural data obtained from the MAO, including crop yield records, market prices, and disaster damage reports. The developed system will be evaluated specifically on functional suitability, usability, and reliability using the ISO/IEC 25010 standard**,** with respondents comprising selected farmers, IT experts, and MAO administrators.

Limitations: The study will not include real-time hardware integration, such as IoT-based soil sensors or automated weather stations. It will not feature live, API-based integration with external or national-level agricultural databases; all system logic will rely on locally inputted and historical data rather than highly advanced, autonomous machine learning models that require massive external datasets.

Definition of Terms

For a clearer understanding of the study, the following terms are defined conceptually and operationally:

Agricultural Decision Support System – A software-based system designed to assist farmers, associations, and agricultural administrators in making data-driven farming, financial, and supply chain decisions.

Crop Saturation – A condition where excessive, simultaneous planting of a specific crop leads to a high risk of oversupply within the local market, potentially resulting in price drops and crop dumping.

Digital Logbook – A centralized digital recording system used by farmers to consistently document agronomic events, such as fertilizer application and daily farm maintenance.

Financial Modeling Tool – A system feature that calculates an economic risk assessment by comparing the projected profit margins of recommended crops against saturated ones, while tracking actual input costs to compute the final Profit and Loss (P\&L).

Harvest Synchronization – The logistical process used within the 

system to schedule and stagger planting and harvesting timelines across multiple farms, preventing the simultaneous dumping of produce into the market.

Incident Reporting Mechanism – A dedicated feature allowing farmers to immediately log and report calamity-induced crop losses, which the MAO uses to verify and expedite government subsidy allocations.

Intercropping (Mix-and-Match) – A recommended planting strategy where multiple complementary crops are planted together within the same land area to optimize land productivity and mitigate the financial risk of relying on a single crop.

ISO/IEC 25010 Standard – An international standard for software product quality used in this study to evaluate the developed system, specifically measuring its functional suitability, usability, and reliability based on end-user feedback.

Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO) – The local government unit responsible for overseeing agricultural programs, verifying calamity reports, and managing the local supply chain dashboard provided by the system.

Supply Chain Dashboard – A visual interface utilized by the MAO and Associations that provides forward-looking market supply 

projections and identifies alternative market channels or buy-back opportunities for predicted surpluses.

CHAPTER 2 REVIEW OF RELATED STUDIES

Review of Existing and Related Studies

*Current Systems*

In many developing agricultural municipalities like Tubungan, the prevailing information systems are largely informal, traditional, and heavily reliant on manual processes. At the farmer level, decision-making regarding crop selection and planting schedules is primarily heuristic based on historical memory, weather lore, or the imitation of neighboring farms. This traditional approach lacks real-time data integration, leaving farmers vulnerable to localized oversupply and the subsequent depreciation of crop market value\[1\]. Financial forecasting is similarly informal, often calculated mentally or recorded in physical ledgers, making it difficult for farmers to accurately project Return on Investment (ROI) or assess the financial risk of input costs (e.g., fertilizers, seeds, labor) before the planting season begins.

At the institutional level, Local Government Units (LGUs) and the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO) typically utilize traditional data-gathering methods. The baseline for agricultural monitoring in the Philippines is often tied to

paper-based surveys and manual data entry into the Registry System for Basic Sectors in Agriculture (RSBSA). Because data collection and consolidation take time, the MAO lacks a real-time, dynamic dashboard to monitor active planting trends. Consequently, they are unable to issue timely advisories to farmers regarding potential market oversaturation of specific crops \[2\]. The absence of a digital, interconnected feedback loop between the farmers' planting intentions and the MAO's monitoring capabilities perpetuates the cycle of supply chain inefficiencies.

*Related Systems or Solutions*

Several technological solutions have been developed to address agricultural inefficiencies, though most function as  
standalone tools rather than fully integrated ecosystems.

Locally, the Department of Science and Technology (DOST) developed Project SARAI (Smarter Approaches to Reinvigorate Agriculture as an Industry in the Philippines). SARAI provides crop advisories, weather forecasts, and yield estimates using geographic information systems (GIS) and remote sensing. However, its primary focus is on maximizing yield and managing

weather risks rather than market supply chain governance and financial forecasting for individual farmers \[3\].

Another notable system is the International Rice Research Institute's (IRRI) Rice Crop Manager (RCM). It is a web-based decision support tool that provides personalized crop and nutrient management recommendations to farmers via SMS or printed outputs. While highly effective for crop planning and optimizing fertilizer use to save costs, RCM is crop-specific (primarily for rice) and does not possess a market-governance module to prevent oversupply of various high-value crops \[4\].

Globally, platforms like Plantix utilize AI to diagnose crop diseases, while market-linkage apps like e-Kadiwa in the Philippines attempt to connect farmers directly to consumers to bypass middlemen. However, these systems are reactive. They address issues *during* cultivation or *after* harvest. Your proposed Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System differentiates  itself  by  being  proactive—integrating pre-planting financial forecasting and LGU-level market governance to preemptively stop oversupply before seeds are even sown.

*Related Studies*

Recent academic studies highlight the critical role of

predictive analytics and Spatial Decision Support Systems

(SDSS) in transforming agriculture. A study by Lioutas *et al.*

on agricultural digitalization emphasizes that Decision

Support Systems (DSS) are most effective when they combine agronomic data with economic models. The researchers noted that while farmers are willing to adopt digital tools, the tools must provide clear, tangible financial forecasts to  
justify the change in their traditional practices \[5\]. This supports your system's inclusion of a financial forecasting module.

Furthermore, literature on the agricultural supply chain

frequently discusses the "Bullwhip Effect" and the "Cobweb Model" of economic theory, where delayed information causes extreme fluctuations in supply and demand. Research by van Dijk *et al.* demonstrates that establishing a centralized  
data-sharing platform at the municipal level allows

authorities to apply predictive analytics to upcoming harvest volumes \[6\]. By mapping planned crop distributions

geospatially, LGUs can visually identify clusters of similar crops and intervene through extension workers. Incorporating geospatial insights into agricultural DSS has been proven to enhance land-use optimization, ensuring that crop varieties are distributed efficiently across different barangays based on both soil suitability and market demand \[7\].

By combining the predictive yield modeling discussed in

spatial studies with the economic governance principles of supply chain management, your proposed system effectively bridges the gap between agricultural production and market absorption.

CHAPTER 3 RESEARCH DESIGN AND METHODOLOGY

Description of the Proposed Study

This study proposes the development of an Integrated

Agricultural Decision Support System that assists farmers and agricultural administrators in optimizing crop planning and  
market supply coordination. The system will provide predictive crop recommendations, financial forecasting tools, and a  
supply chain monitoring dashboard designed to mitigate the risks of market saturation.

The system will enable farmers to input land area, crop

preferences, and production costs. Based on these inputs, the system will analyze historical market data and agricultural  
production trends to recommend optimal crop strategies. It will also simulate potential profits and losses to help  
farmers evaluate the financial feasibility of different planting options.

Additionally, the system will allow the Municipal Agriculture Office to monitor upcoming harvest projections across  
different farmers and identify possible market surpluses. This enables agricultural administrators to implement proactive

strategies such as connecting farmers with institutional buyers or initiating surplus redistribution programs.

Methods and Proposed Enhancements

*Topic Title*

Research Design This study will utilize a developmental research design, which involves the systematic study of designing, developing, and evaluating an instructional program, process, or product that must meet criteria of  
internal consistency and effectiveness. Specifically, this

research focuses on the applied development of an Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System. The design is intended to solve practical problems in crop planning, financial  
forecasting, and market supply chain governance by replacing manual, fragmented processes with a centralized, data-driven digital solution.

System Development Life Cycle (SDLC) To ensure a structured and iterative development process, the study will adopt the Agile Software Development Life Cycle methodology. Agile is highly suited for this project as it allows for continuous  
improvement and flexibility based on user feedback from local farmers and the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO).

The Agile process for this study will be divided into the following phases:

* Requirements Planning: Identifying the specific needs for crop recommendation, financial forecasting, and surplus

  management through stakeholder consultations.

* Design and Prototyping: Creating wireframes and mockups for the farmer's mobile interface and the MAO's

  centralized dashboard.

* Iterative Development (Sprints): Coding the system in functional modules (e.g., completing the Financial

  Forecasting tool first, testing it, and then moving to the Agronomic Logbook).

* Testing and Deployment: Conducting continuous testing during development, followed by deployment to a cloud server for accessibility.

Data Gathering Methods To accurately model the algorithms and ensure the system meets user requirements, data will be  
collected through the following methods:

* Interviews: Semi-structured interviews will be conducted with MAO officials and agricultural technologists to

  understand current supply chain bottlenecks, local demand thresholds, and existing disaster assessment protocols.

* Surveys: Questionnaires will be distributed to members of local farmer associations to assess their technical

readiness, current financial tracking habits, and the primary factors influencing their crop selection.

* Document Analysis: The system's predictive models will be grounded by analyzing secondary data, including

  historical market pricing trends, seasonal planting calendars, and soil suitability matrices provided by agricultural agencies.

Proposed System Architecture and Enhancements *(This is where you will insert the excellent 4-point breakdown you provided earlier, detailing the Predictive Crop Recommendation,*  
*Financial Forecasting Tool, Supply Chain Dashboard, and Digital Logbook).*

Development Tools The development of the system will utilize a modern technology stack to ensure scalability, security, and  
cross-platform accessibility:

* Front-End: Flutter (Dart) \- Cross-platform mobile app for farmers and buyers (works on Android, iOS, Web) Material Design UI with custom theming

* Back-End: Supabase \- Backend-as-a-Service providing:

  Authentication (email/password login) PostgreSQL database Real-time subscriptions Row Level Security (RLS) policies Edge Functions (serverless)

* Database: PostgreSQL (via Supabase) \- Storing: User

  profiles (farmers, admins, MAO, buyers) Crop data and saturation records Market prices Crop listings and

  reservations Historical planting records

* Cloud Infrastructure: Supabase Cloud \- Hosted PostgreSQL with automatic backups, high availability, and global CDN

* Key Packages Used: supabase\_flutter \- Supabase SDK for Flutter intl \- Date/number formatting fl\_chart \- Charts and graphs (for analytics)

System Evaluation and Testing Once the prototype is developed, it will undergo rigorous evaluation to ensure it meets  
standard software quality metrics. The system will be

evaluated using the ISO/IEC 25010 Software Product Quality Model.

A panel of IT experts, MAO personnel, and selected farmers will test the system and evaluate it based on criteria such as:

* Functional Suitability: Does the harvest synchronization algorithm work as intended?

* Usability: Is the mobile interface intuitive for farmers with varying levels of technical literacy?

* Performance Efficiency: Can the system handle data inputs from multiple users simultaneously without crashing?

* Security: Are the financial and farm data of the users protected?

Components and Design

*System Architecture*

\[Diagram/ Model and relevant description\]

The system architecture follows a centralized cloud-based

model designed to facilitate real-time data exchange between three primary stakeholder groups: the Farmer Production &  
Planning Group, the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO), and the Institutional Market Group.  
*Software Architecture*

\[Diagram/ Model and relevant description\]

*Database Design*

\[ERD in 3NF, NoSQL visualization, or Data Dictionary\]

The database utilizes a PostgreSQL schema hosted via Supabase, structured in Third Normal Form (3NF) to ensure data integrity and reduce redundancy. Key entities include:

* Profiles and Auth: Manages user roles (Farmer, Admin, MAO) and authentication.

* Farming Projects & Saturation Records: Tracks specific

  planting activities, companion crops, and expected yields to calculate localized saturation levels.

* Economic Tracking: The Expenses and Production Reports tables allow for detailed Profit and Loss (P\&L)

  calculations.

* Governance & Support: Specialized tables for Calamity Reports and Equipment Rental facilitate government

  subsidy verification and resource sharing among farmers.

*Procedural Design*

\[ALL Flowchart\]

The authentication procedure is designed as a secure gateway that directs users to their respective functional dashboards based on their assigned roles.

* **Validation**: The system validates user credentials through

  # **Supabase Auth**.

* **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**: Upon successful login, the system checks if the user's role is a "Farmer" or

  "Admin/MAO".

* **Redirection**: Farmers are routed to the

  **FarmerDashboardScreen**, while administrators are directed to the **MaoAdminDashboard** to ensure relevant data access and security.

*Object-Oriented Design*

\[UML Diagrams, not limited to Use Case, Class, Activity, Sequence, State, Deployment\]

The Level 0 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) establishes the

boundaries of the **IADSS System**, illustrating its role as a

central mediator for agricultural information. It defines the high-level bidirectional data exchange between the system and three external entities: the **Farmer**, the **MAO**, and the

# **Association**.

*Process Design*

The proposed Level 1 Data Flow Diagram (DFD) will decompose

the system's macro-level context into four primary functional processes to illustrate the precise routing, storage, and  
transformation of agricultural and market data. Process 1.0,

Log Planting Intent & Report Damage (Mobile App), will manage primary user interactions by ingesting planting intents, daily agronomic logbooks, and calamity reports directly from the  
Farmer Production & Planning Group. This module will provide immediate alternative crop suggestions to the farmers while routing the raw farmer reports and data inward.

Concurrently, Process 2.0, Consolidate & Store Data (Central Hub), will orchestrate multi-stakeholder inputs by receiving the aforementioned farmer data, alongside virtual inventory  
reservation agreements from the Institutional Market Group and intervention directives from the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO). This hub will systematically cleanse and write these  
consolidated agricultural records into the central D1 Central Cloud Database, establishing a unified source of truth. To  
systematically mitigate market oversupply, Process 3.0,

Predictive Planting & Financial Forecasts (Automated Engine), will extract historical market prices and crop saturation data from D1 to algorithmically generate targeted pre-planting risk

alerts and mix-and-match (intercropping) strategies for the farmers.

Furthermore, Process 4.0, Perform Geospatial & Supply

Analytics (Admin Dashboard), will function as a synthesized decision-support engine for governance; it will fetch  
aggregated geospatial and supply chain data from D1 to compute and render dynamic visualizations. Finally, overarching  
municipal oversight will be facilitated by routing these visualizations—specifically production cluster saturation  
mapping and supply synchronization alerts—directly to the MAO to streamline post-harvest surplus redistribution and subsidy verification.

Methodology

*System Development Life Cycle*

\[This section should outline and describe in detail

research development activities, including the methods used.\]

CHAPTER 4 RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

Implementation

*Development Tools*

The proposed Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System was successfully developed and implemented using a cross-platform Flutter framework integrated with Supabase cloud services. The system was designed to provide predictive crop recommendation, financial forecasting, harvest synchronization, supply chain monitoring, and digital agronomic logging functionalities for farmers, Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO) personnel, and agricultural associations.

The implementation process followed an Agile Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), allowing iterative development, continuous testing, and refinement based on stakeholder feedback. The completed system supports real-time data synchronization, cloud-based storage, and role-based access control for different user categories.

The system was deployed through a cloud-hosted environment to ensure accessibility across multiple devices such as smartphones, tablets, laptops, and desktop computers. During implementation, the researchers ensured that the developed application remained lightweight, responsive, and compatible with low-to-mid-range devices commonly used by farmers and local agricultural offices.

![][image2]

Input and output of the study

The developed system accepts several agricultural and financial inputs from farmers and administrators. These include:

* Farmer profile information  
* Land area and location  
* Crop preferences  
* Historical crop planting records  
* Fertilizer and labor expenses  
* Expected harvest schedules  
* Disaster incident reports  
* Market price information

After processing the collected data, the system generates the following outputs:

* Recommended crop strategies  
* Crop saturation warnings  
* Financial forecasting reports  
* Profit and Loss (P\&L) summaries  
* Harvest synchronization schedules  
* Supply chain monitoring dashboards  
* Disaster assessment reports  
* Oversupply alerts and redistribution recommendations

The outputs are presented through interactive dashboards, graphs, charts, and tabular reports for easier interpretation by farmers and MAO personnel.

# Result interpretation and Analysis

The developed Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System successfully demonstrated the capability to assist farmers and agricultural administrators in making data-driven decisions regarding crop planning, financial forecasting, and supply chain coordination.

The recommendation module effectively analyzed crop saturation levels, land suitability, and seasonal conditions to generate crop suggestions and harvest timelines. Through predictive analysis, the system was able to identify potential oversupply situations before planting activities commenced.

The financial forecasting tool enabled farmers to estimate projected profits and compare economic risks between recommended crops and already saturated crops. Farmers were also able to monitor actual expenses and calculate post-harvest Profit and Loss (P\&L), improving financial transparency and planning.

The supply chain dashboard provided the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO) with forward-looking production projections and crop distribution visualizations. This allowed administrators to identify possible harvest congestion and proactively coordinate redistribution or alternative market linkage programs.

Meanwhile, the digital agronomic logbook and incident reporting mechanism streamlined the documentation of farm activities and calamity-induced losses. The reporting process significantly reduced manual paperwork and improved subsidy verification efficiency.

Throughout implementation and testing, the researchers observed that users quickly adapted to the system interface due to its simple navigation structure and responsive design.

# System Evaluation Results

The developed system was evaluated using the ISO/IEC 25010 Software Product Quality Model. The evaluation focused on the following quality characteristics:

* Functional Suitability  
* Usability  
* Reliability

The respondents consisted of selected farmers, MAO personnel, agricultural staff, and IT experts.

## 

## Functional Suitability

The respondents evaluated the system as highly functional in providing crop recommendations, financial forecasting, supply monitoring, and disaster reporting capabilities. The predictive recommendation module and supply synchronization dashboard were identified as the most valuable features.

## Usability

The system obtained positive usability feedback due to its intuitive interface, readable layout, and simplified navigation process. Farmers with limited technical background were able to operate the mobile application with minimal assistance.

## Reliability

The system demonstrated stable performance during testing, with successful handling of simultaneous user inputs and real-time synchronization across cloud databases. Minor connectivity delays were observed during unstable internet conditions; however, the application remained operational without major system failures.

Overall, the evaluation results indicate that the developed Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System met the required software quality standards based on ISO/IEC 25010 and is considered acceptable for deployment and utilization within local agricultural communities.

Results Interpretation and Analysis

*Topic Title*

The developed Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System successfully demonstrated the capability to assist farmers and agricultural administrators in making data-driven decisions regarding crop planning, financial forecasting, and supply chain coordination.

The recommendation module effectively analyzed crop saturation levels, land suitability, and seasonal conditions to generate crop suggestions and harvest timelines. Through predictive analysis, the system was able to identify potential oversupply situations before planting activities commenced.

The financial forecasting tool enabled farmers to estimate projected profits and compare economic risks between recommended crops and already saturated crops. Farmers were also able to monitor actual expenses and calculate post-harvest Profit and Loss (P\&L), improving financial transparency and planning.

The supply chain dashboard provided the Municipal Agriculture Office (MAO) with forward-looking production projections and crop distribution visualizations. This allowed administrators to identify possible harvest congestion and proactively coordinate redistribution or alternative market linkage programs.

Meanwhile, the digital agronomic logbook and incident reporting mechanism streamlined the documentation of farm activities and calamity-induced losses. The reporting process significantly reduced manual paperwork and improved subsidy verification efficiency.

Throughout implementation and testing, the researchers observed that users quickly adapted to the system interface due to its simple navigation structure and responsive design.

System Evaluation Results

*Topic Title*

The developed system was evaluated using the ISO/IEC 25010 Software Product Quality Model. The evaluation focused on the following quality characteristics:

* Functional Suitability  
* Usability  
* Reliability

The respondents consisted of selected farmers, MAO personnel, agricultural staff, and IT experts.

## Functional Suitability

The respondents evaluated the system as highly functional in providing crop recommendations, financial forecasting, supply monitoring, and disaster reporting capabilities. The predictive recommendation module and supply synchronization dashboard were identified as the most valuable features.

## Usability

The system obtained positive usability feedback due to its intuitive interface, readable layout, and simplified navigation process. Farmers with limited technical background were able to 

operate the mobile application with minimal assistance.

## Reliability

The system demonstrated stable performance during testing, with successful handling of simultaneous user inputs and real-time synchronization across cloud databases. Minor connectivity delays were observed during unstable internet conditions; however, the application remained operational without major system failures.

Overall, the evaluation results indicate that the developed Integrated Agricultural Decision Support System met the required software quality standards based on ISO/IEC 25010 and is considered acceptable for deployment and utilization within local agricultural communities.

CHAPTER 5 SUMMARY, CONCLUSIONS, AND RECOMMENDATIONS

Summary of the Proposed Study, Design, and Implementation

*Topic Title*

\[Content Here\]

Summary of Findings

References

1. *R. M. Briones, "Transforming Philippine Agriculture: During COVID-19 and Beyond," Philippine Institute for*

*Development Studies (PIDS), Quezon City, Philippines, Discussion Paper Series No. 2020-36, 2020\.*

2. *R. Mendoza et al., "Assessing the digital readiness of local government units in the delivery of agricultural*

*extension services in the Philippines," Asian Journal of*

*Agriculture and Development, vol. 18, no. 2, pp. 1-18, 2021\.*

3. *J. A. Cruz et al., "Smarter Approaches to Reinvigorate Agriculture as an Industry in the Philippines (Project*

*SARAI): Framework and Implementation," Journal of*

*Environmental Science and Management, vol. 21, no. 1, 2018\.*

4. *R. J. Buresh et al., "Rice Crop Manager: A web-based decision support tool for precision farming in Asia,"*

*Precision Agriculture, vol. 20, pp. 1-15, 2019\.*

5. *E. D. Lioutas, C. Charatsari, and M. De Rosa,*

*"Digitalization in agriculture: A way to solve the food*

*problem or a trolley problem?," Technology in Society, vol. 67, p. 101744, 2021\.*

6. *M. van Dijk et al., "A meta-analysis of projected global food demand and population at risk of hunger for the period 2010–2050," Nature Food, vol. 1, no. 7, pp. 414-422, 2020\.*

7. *Z. Zhai, J. F. Martínez, V. Beltran, and N. L. Martínez, "Decision support systems for agriculture 4.0: Survey and*

*challenges," Computers and Electronics in Agriculture, vol. 170, p. 105256, 2020\.*

\[1\] Placeholder Image | Elementor Developers. \[Online\].

Available:https://developers.elementor.com/docs/hooks/place holder-image/. \[Accessed: Jan. 15, 2024\].

Here are examples of IEEE citation styles for various types of sources:

# **Journal Article:**

Format:

\[1\] A. Author, "Title of the Article," Title of the Journal, vol. x, no. x, pp. xxx-xxx, Month, Year.  
Example:

\[1\] J. Smith, "Advancements in Artificial Intelligence,"

IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems, vol. 28, no. 3, pp. 512-525, Mar. 2021\.

# **Conference Proceedings:**

Format:

\[1\] A. Author, "Title of the Paper," in Title of the Conference, Year, pp. xxx-xxx.  
Example:

\[1\] R. Johnson, "Improving Energy Efficiency in Buildings," in Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on  
Sustainable Energy Technologies, 2019, pp. 45-52.

# **Book:**

Format:

\[1\] A. Author, Title of the Book. Publisher, Year.

Example:

\[1\] K. Johnson, Introduction to Machine Learning. Springer, 2020\.

# **Website:**

Format:

\[1\] "Title of the Webpage," Title of the Website. \[Online\].

Available: URL. \[Accessed: Month Day, Year\]. Example:  
\[1\] "About Us," OpenAI. \[Online\]. Available:

https://openai.com/about. \[Accessed: Jan. 15, 2024\].

# **News Article:**

Format:

\[1\] A. Author, "Title of the Article," Title of the Newspaper/Magazine, Month Day, Year.  
Example:

\[1\] J. Doe, "New Study Shows Effects of Climate Change," The New York Times, Jul. 5, 2023\.

# **Unpublished Thesis:**

Format:

\[1\] A. Author, "Title of the Thesis," Master's/Ph.D.

thesis, Abbrev. Dept., Abbrev. Univ., City, Country, Year. Example:  
\[1\] S. Johnson, "Analysis of Cryptographic Algorithms," Ph.D. thesis, Dept. of Computer Science, Univ. of  
California, Berkeley, USA, 2020\.

**Appendix \#** Disclaimer

This research project and its corresponding documentation entitled “\[Title Here\]” is submitted to the College of  
Information and Communications Technology, West Visayas

State University, in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree, \[Degree Program\]. It is the product of our  
own work, except where indicated text.

We hereby grant the College of Information and

Communications Technology permission to freely use, publish in local or international journal/conferences, reproduce, or distribute publicly the paper and electronic copies of this software project and its corresponding documentation in  
whole or in part, provided that we are acknowledged.
